text: Gas separations using porous materials are of immense economic significance, yet they present a distinct challenge in materials design: the need to precisely tune adsorbent properties to achieve high selectivity. Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) have emerged as a versatile class of porous materials, offering nearly limitless architectural possibilities for engineering specific functionalities. Given the vast landscape of potential framework structures, quantum mechanical simulations serve as an essential tool for identifying and prioritizing the most promising materials before synthesis. In this work, we use these computational methods to predict a novel MOF tailored for the separation of dinitrogen from methane—a notoriously difficult process critical for the purification of natural gas. We demonstrate that the incorporation of open V(II) sites into the framework results in a significantly higher enthalpy of adsorption for dinitrogen compared to methane. This selectivity is driven by strong, specific back-bonding interactions with dinitrogen that are absent in the case of methane, providing a robust mechanism for efficient gas separation.
